<!-- generated from maestro item 273 — do not edit; the board item is the source of truth -->
# llm.get_model() picks the oldest alias in a tier

- **Kind**: bug
- **Date**: 2026-07-21
- **Requested by**: Ian (found while scoping maestro item #271, the knowledge-ingestion curator)
- **Rules**: django-mojo-build-conventions

## Description

`mojo/helpers/llm.py` resolves a use-case tier ("powerful" / "general" / "fast") to a concrete model ID. Two defects in that resolution, plus one piece of dead code:

1. **`_pick_best_model` returns the oldest alias when aliases tie on length.** It sorts `candidates` by `(len(id), created_at)` **ascending**, then returns the first alias it finds in that order. Every current Opus alias is exactly 15 characters — `claude-opus-4-8`, `claude-opus-4-7`, `claude-opus-4-6`, `claude-opus-4-5`, `claude-opus-4-1`, `claude-opus-4-0` — so the length key ties and the tiebreak is `created_at` ascending: **oldest wins**. `get_model("powerful")` returns a deprecated Opus 4.0/4.1 instead of the current one.
2. **`_FALLBACKS["powerful"]` is a Sonnet.** It maps to `claude-sonnet-4-20250514` — wrong tier entirely, and a deprecated dated snapshot (Sonnet 4 retires 2026-06-15). `_FALLBACKS["general"]` points at the same deprecated snapshot.
3. **`_FAMILY_ORDER` is dead.** Defined at the top of the module, never referenced. It also hardcodes the `-4` generation, so it would have gone stale regardless.

## Context

**This is latent, not live — but it is one model release away from being live, on the hot path.**

Every current production caller resolves `get_model("general")` → the sonnet family, and sonnet is currently correct *by accident*: `claude-sonnet-5` is 15 chars while `claude-sonnet-4-6` / `-4-5` / `-4-0` are 17, so the shortest-first sort happens to surface the newest. No production caller asks for `"powerful"` today, so defect (1) is currently unobserved.

That coincidence breaks on the next Sonnet release. When `claude-sonnet-6` ships it will be 15 characters — a length tie with `claude-sonnet-5` — and the ascending `created_at` tiebreak will pin every assistant, incident-handler, and async-job call to **Sonnet 5** indefinitely, silently. The naming pattern is the whole problem: the heuristic only works when the newer model has a *shorter* ID, which holds across generations (`claude-sonnet-4-6` → `claude-sonnet-5`) and fails within one (`claude-opus-4-5` → `-4-6` → `-4-7` → `-4-8`).

Two further reasons to fix it now rather than after it bites:

- **The existing test cannot catch it.** `tests/test_helpers/llm.py` `SAMPLE_MODELS` contains exactly one opus alias (`claude-opus-4-6`) plus one dated variant, so `test_pick_best_model_opus` passes without ever exercising the tiebreak. The suite is green and the bug is invisible to it.
- **maestro is about to depend on the `"powerful"` tier.** Maestro board item #271 (knowledge-ingestion quality gate) plans to use `mojo.helpers.llm` for an LLM curator pass and wants the most capable model. It would silently get a deprecated Opus.

Note that `get_model()` short-circuits on an explicit `LLM_ADMIN_MODEL` / `LLM_HANDLER_MODEL` pin before reaching any of this, so pinning is the interim workaround for consumers.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `get_model("powerful")` returns the newest available Opus-tier alias, not the oldest.
- [ ] Tier resolution is correct for aliases that tie on ID length — the fix does not rely on the newer model having a shorter name.
- [ ] `get_model("general")` still resolves to the current Sonnet, and keeps doing so when a same-length successor alias exists alongside the incumbent.
- [ ] `_FALLBACKS["powerful"]` names an Opus-tier model; `_FALLBACKS` contains no model that is deprecated or retired at the time of the fix.
- [ ] `_FAMILY_ORDER` is either used or removed — no dead constant naming a specific generation.
- [ ] **Regression test** (per `django-mojo-build-conventions`): a fixture containing at least two same-length aliases in one family with different `created_at` values. It must fail on the current code and pass on the fix. The existing single-alias `SAMPLE_MODELS` does not exercise this — extend it or add a dedicated fixture.
- [ ] Docs updated if the resolution order or the tier→family mapping changes (`docs/django_developer/helpers/llm.md` documents the current three-step order).
- [ ] **(Added during scoping)** The Redis model cache actually populates — `_fetch_models_from_api` returns JSON-serializable dicts, and a serialization failure logs instead of being swallowed.

## Investigation

- **What exists**:
  - `mojo/helpers/llm.py` — `_FALLBACKS`, `_FAMILY_ORDER`, `_USE_TO_FAMILY`, `_pick_best_model`, `get_model`, `get_models` (24h Redis cache), `call`, `ask`.
  - `tests/test_helpers/llm.py` — `SAMPLE_MODELS` fixture and the `test_pick_best_model_*` cases.
  - `docs/django_developer/helpers/llm.md` — documents the pin → auto-detect → fallback order.
- **What changes**: the ranking inside `_pick_best_model`, the `_FALLBACKS` table, and the `_FAMILY_ORDER` constant. Public signatures (`get_model(use)`) should not need to change.
- **Constraints**:
  - Model IDs are opaque strings from `/v1/models`; the fix should not hardcode a version list that goes stale on the next release — that is the failure mode being fixed.
  - `created_at` is available on every entry from the Models API and is the reliable recency signal; the current code has it and sorts the wrong way.
  - Dated snapshots (`claude-opus-4-1-20250805`) must keep losing to aliases (`claude-opus-4-8`) — the alias always tracks the latest build. The existing date-suffix detection is functionally correct but reads as a typo: `any(ch.isdigit() and len(part) == 8 for part in mid.split("-") for ch in [part])` binds `ch` to `part` itself, so it works while looking like it doesn't. Worth rewriting for legibility while in here.
  - **Tier vocabulary may be too narrow to fix in isolation**: `_USE_TO_FAMILY` maps only to the substrings `opus` / `sonnet` / `haiku`, so no tier can ever return `claude-fable-5` or `claude-mythos-5`. Decide during scoping whether "powerful" should be able to reach those, or whether that needs a fourth tier — either way, call it out rather than leaving it implicit.
- **Related files**: `mojo/helpers/llm.py`, `tests/test_helpers/llm.py`, `docs/django_developer/helpers/llm.md`
- **Callers that would be affected** (all currently resolve `"general"`; listed so the fix can be sanity-checked against real usage): `mojo/apps/assistant/services/agent.py:1211` and `:1438`, `mojo/apps/assistant/services/tools/files.py:178`, `mojo/apps/assistant/services/memory.py:704`, `mojo/apps/incident/handlers/llm_agent.py:29`, `mojo/apps/jobs/asyncjobs.py:100`.
- **Out of scope**:
  - Adding structured-output (`output_config`) or Message Batches support to `llm.call()` — both wanted by maestro #271, both separate items.
  - The API-key resolution path (`get_api_key()`), which reads only `LLM_ADMIN_API_KEY` → `LLM_HANDLER_API_KEY` with no environment-variable fallback. Separate concern; being verified independently.
  - Prompt-caching behavior in `call()`.

---

## Scoping Findings (2026-07-22)

Everything below was verified by running code, not by reading alone.

### 1. The bug reproduces exactly as described

Ran the current `_pick_best_model` against a realistic model list (all six Opus
aliases + their dated snapshots, four Sonnet entries, one Haiku):

```
opus   -> claude-opus-4-0      # oldest Opus in the list
sonnet -> claude-sonnet-5      # correct, by accident
haiku  -> claude-haiku-4-5     # correct
```

Then added a hypothetical `claude-sonnet-6` (created 2026-11-01):

```
sonnet -> claude-sonnet-5      # stays pinned to the incumbent, silently
```

Both failure modes in the report are real.

### 2. NEW — the 24h Redis model cache has never worked

Not in the original report, found while reading `_fetch_models_from_api`.
Verified against the installed SDK (`anthropic 0.96.0`):

- `ModelInfo.created_at` is typed `datetime.datetime`, and plain
  `model.model_dump()` returns a **datetime object**, not a string.
- `_cache_set` therefore hits `json.dumps(models)` → `TypeError: Object of type
  datetime is not JSON serializable`.
- That call sits inside `try: ... except Exception: pass`, so the failure is
  invisible. **The Redis cache is never written**, `_cache_get` never finds
  anything there, and every worker falls back to its own in-memory copy —
  re-fetching from Anthropic on every process start instead of once per day
  across the fleet.

This is in scope for this item (confirmed with Ian). It also matters *for the
ranking fix*: `created_at` is a datetime on the live path and a string on the
cached/test path, so the new sort key must not assume one or the other.

### 3. Tier vocabulary — decided: leave the three tiers alone

`_USE_TO_FAMILY` keeps mapping `powerful→opus`, `general→sonnet`, `fast→haiku`.
Rationale: repointing a tier at a different family silently changes the model
every existing caller receives — a behavior change, not a bug fix. Anything
outside the three families stays reachable via an explicit `model=` argument or
an `LLM_ADMIN_MODEL` pin. This limit gets documented; adding a fourth tier is a
separate item if it's ever wanted.

### 4. Two drive-by defects in the same module

- `logger.warning("...: %s", str(e)[:200])` at `mojo/helpers/llm.py:123` does not
  interpolate. `logit`'s logger joins its `*args` with newlines
  (`mojo/helpers/logit.py:241-258`) — `%`-style formatting is not supported, so
  this logs the literal `%s` and the value on separate lines. One-line fix.
- `_USE_TO_FAMILY.get(use, "sonnet")` silently accepts any unknown tier string
  and hands back a Sonnet. A typo (`get_model("strong")`) is undetectable.

---

## Plan

### Objective

`get_model(tier)` returns the newest model in that tier, determined from the
API's own `created_at` field rather than from the shape of the model's name, so
it stays correct across future releases regardless of how Anthropic names them.
Fallbacks name current models in the right tier. The shared model cache works.

### Implementation steps

#### Step 1 — `mojo/helpers/llm.py`: replace the ranking

Delete `_FAMILY_ORDER` (dead, and hardcodes the `-4` generation).

Replace `_pick_best_model` (currently lines 179-209) with a single ranking
function and a `max()`. Three small helpers instead of the sort/loop/re-sort:

```python
def _is_dated_snapshot(model_id):
    """True for IDs ending in a YYYYMMDD build date (claude-opus-4-1-20250805)."""
    tail = model_id.rsplit("-", 1)[-1]
    return len(tail) == 8 and tail.isdigit()


def _version_tuple(model_id):
    """Numeric version parts of an ID: claude-opus-4-8 -> (4, 8)."""
    return tuple(
        int(part) for part in model_id.split("-")
        if part.isdigit() and len(part) != 8
    )


def _created_at_key(model):
    """created_at as a sortable string — the API sends a datetime, the cache a string."""
    value = model.get("created_at") or ""
    if hasattr(value, "isoformat"):
        value = value.isoformat()
    return str(value)


def _rank_key(model):
    """
    Preference key for a model entry — bigger is better.

    1. An alias beats a dated snapshot: the alias tracks the latest build.
    2. Newest created_at wins. This is the API's own recency signal, and the
       only one that survives Anthropic changing how models are named.
    3. Version number, then the ID itself, purely so ties are deterministic.

    Do NOT rank by ID length. Every alias within a generation is the same
    length (claude-opus-4-1 / claude-opus-4-8), so length carries no recency
    information — that assumption is what this function is replacing.
    """
    model_id = model.get("id", "")
    return (
        0 if _is_dated_snapshot(model_id) else 1,
        _created_at_key(model),
        _version_tuple(model_id),
        model_id,
    )


def _pick_best_model(models, family_keyword):
    """
    Pick the best model for a family keyword (opus/sonnet/haiku).

    Returns the family's newest alias, or its newest dated snapshot when the
    family has no alias. None when nothing matches.
    """
    candidates = [m for m in models if family_keyword in m.get("id", "")]
    if not candidates:
        return None
    return max(candidates, key=_rank_key)["id"]
```

Notes for the builder:
- `_version_tuple` compares correctly across generations because tuples compare
  element-wise: `(5,) > (4, 8)`. It is only a tiebreaker for entries sharing a
  timestamp — `created_at` is the primary signal.
- The two keys back each other up. If `created_at` were ever missing on every
  entry, the version tuple still yields the right answer; if the naming scheme
  changes shape, `created_at` still does. Both have to fail to produce a wrong
  result.
- `_is_dated_snapshot` replaces the `for ch in [part]` expression. Same
  behavior, reads like what it does.
- `.get("id", "")` on the membership filter: the old code used `m.get("id", "")`
  too. Keep it — a malformed entry must not raise.

#### Step 2 — `mojo/helpers/llm.py`: current fallbacks

```python
# Hardcoded fallbacks when the API is unreachable. Aliases, not dated
# snapshots — an alias follows the latest build and doesn't retire on a
# snapshot's schedule. Revisit when a new generation ships.
_FALLBACKS = {
    "powerful": "claude-opus-4-8",
    "general": "claude-sonnet-5",
    "fast": "claude-haiku-4-5",
}
```

`powerful` moves to the Opus tier, and all three name a current model.

#### Step 3 — `mojo/helpers/llm.py`: unknown tier is no longer silent

In `get_model`, replace `family_keyword = _USE_TO_FAMILY.get(use, "sonnet")`:

```python
    family_keyword = _USE_TO_FAMILY.get(use)
    if not family_keyword:
        logger.warning(f"Unknown model tier '{use}' — using 'general'")
        use = "general"
        family_keyword = _USE_TO_FAMILY[use]
```

Reassigning `use` also fixes step 3 of the resolution order, so an unknown tier
falls back to the general model rather than to `_FALLBACKS.get(use, ...)`'s
default by coincidence. Behavior for the three known tiers is unchanged.

#### Step 4 — `mojo/helpers/llm.py`: make the model cache actually cache

In `_fetch_models_from_api`, both `models.append(...)` calls (lines 115 and 119)
become:

```python
                models.append(model.model_dump(mode="json"))
```

`mode="json"` serializes `created_at` to an ISO string, which is what
`_cache_get` already returns from Redis and what the test fixtures use — one
shape everywhere.

In `_cache_set`, separate "can't serialize" (a bug) from "no Redis" (normal):

```python
def _cache_set(models):
    """Store models in Redis (with TTL) and in-memory."""
    _mem_cache["models"] = models
    _mem_cache["fetched_at"] = time.time()
    try:
        payload = json.dumps(models)
    except TypeError as err:
        # Would otherwise disable the shared cache silently — say so.
        logger.warning(f"Model list is not JSON-serializable, skipping Redis cache: {err}")
        return
    try:
        from mojo.helpers.redis import get_connection
        r = get_connection()
        r.setex(CACHE_KEY, CACHE_TTL, payload)
    except Exception as err:
        # Redis being unavailable is expected — the in-memory cache covers it.
        logger.debug(f"Redis model cache write skipped: {err}")
```

Also fix line 123 while in the file — `logit` joins `*args` with newlines and
does not do `%`-interpolation:

```python
        logger.warning(f"Failed to fetch models from Anthropic API: {str(e)[:200]}")
```

#### Step 5 — `tests/test_helpers/llm.py`

Read `docs/django_developer/testit/Overview.md` first (mandatory per
`.claude/rules/testing.md`). Existing conventions in this file already match:
`@th.django_unit_test()`, `def test_x(opts):`, import inside the function, every
assert carries a message.

**Leave `SAMPLE_MODELS` and the existing assertions untouched.** They stay green
across the change, which is the evidence that nothing regressed. Add a dedicated
fixture instead:

```python
# Aliases that tie on ID length within a family. This is the case the old
# "shortest ID wins, oldest breaks the tie" ranking got backwards.
SAME_LENGTH_ALIAS_MODELS = [
    {"id": "claude-opus-4-1", "created_at": "2025-08-05T00:00:00Z", ...},
    {"id": "claude-opus-4-8", "created_at": "2026-06-15T00:00:00Z", ...},
    {"id": "claude-opus-4-1-20250805", "created_at": "2025-08-05T00:00:00Z", ...},
    {"id": "claude-sonnet-5", "created_at": "2026-05-01T00:00:00Z", ...},
    {"id": "claude-sonnet-6", "created_at": "2026-11-01T00:00:00Z", ...},
]
```

New tests:

| Test | Asserts | Fails today? |
|---|---|---|
| `test_pick_best_model_newest_same_length_alias` | opus → `claude-opus-4-8` | **yes** (returns `claude-opus-4-1`) |
| `test_pick_best_model_next_generation_alias` | sonnet → `claude-sonnet-6` | **yes** (returns `claude-sonnet-5`) |
| `test_pick_best_model_alias_beats_dated_snapshot` | opus alias wins over `-20250805` even though the snapshot's date ties | no — guards the property the fix must preserve |
| `test_pick_best_model_only_dated_snapshots` | family with no alias → newest `created_at` snapshot | no — guards the second branch |
| `test_pick_best_model_ignores_input_order` | same answer with the fixture reversed | no — guards determinism |
| `test_pick_best_model_datetime_created_at` | fixture with real `datetime` values ranks the same as the string fixture | **yes** if the sort key ever assumes `str` |
| `test_fallbacks_are_in_the_right_tier` | `"opus" in _FALLBACKS["powerful"]`, `"sonnet" in ...["general"]`, `"haiku" in ...["fast"]` | **yes** (`powerful` is a Sonnet) |
| `test_fetch_models_returns_json_serializable` | patch `anthropic.Anthropic` with a stub whose page yields **real** `anthropic.types.ModelInfo` objects; assert `json.dumps(llm._fetch_models_from_api(api_key="x"))` succeeds and `created_at` is a `str` | **yes** (`TypeError` today) |

The last one uses a real `ModelInfo` on purpose — a hand-rolled stub with a
`model_dump` of our own would prove nothing about the SDK's actual behavior.
`_fetch_models_from_api` imports `anthropic` inside the function, so
`mock.patch("anthropic.Anthropic")` works in-process. These are plain unit tests
(no `opts.client`), so in-process patching is fine — the server-isolation caveat
in `.claude/rules/testing.md` does not apply.

Tighten one existing test: `test_get_model_fallback` currently accepts
`"opus" in result or "sonnet" in result` for `"powerful"`. Make it require
`"opus"` — true via auto-detect with a key, and via the new fallback without one.

Run: `bin/run_tests --agent -t test_helpers.llm`, then the full default suite
`bin/run_tests --agent`.

**Baseline first** (`.claude/rules/build-baseline.md`): run `bin/run_tests --agent`
before the first edit and record total/passed/failed/skipped in the item.

#### Step 6 — docs

`docs/django_developer/helpers/llm.md`, "Model Selection" section:
- State the tier→family mapping explicitly (powerful=Opus, general=Sonnet,
  fast=Haiku) and that these three are the only families a tier can reach —
  anything else needs `model=` or an `LLM_ADMIN_MODEL` pin.
- Describe how auto-detect ranks: newest `created_at` within the family, alias
  preferred over dated snapshot. Say plainly that ID length is not used.
- Update the sample `model="claude-sonnet-4-6"` in the `call()` example to a
  current model.
- Note that an unrecognized tier logs a warning and resolves as `"general"`.

`docs/django_developer/helpers/llm.md`, "Cache" section: the Redis cache is now
actually populated; note the 24h TTL is shared across workers.

No `docs/web_developer/` change — this helper has no REST surface.

`CHANGELOG.md`: entry under the rolling block — model tier resolution now picks
the newest model by creation date instead of by ID length, fallbacks updated,
shared model cache fixed.

### Design decisions

- **`created_at` as the primary signal, version number as tiebreak.** The bug
  was a naming heuristic; replacing it with a different naming heuristic
  (highest version number) would just move the assumption. `created_at` is data
  the API supplies. The version tuple stays as a deterministic tiebreak and as a
  second, independent signal — considered and rejected making it primary.
- **`max()` over a composite key, not sort → loop → re-sort.** The old code's
  three-phase structure is where the defect hid. One key function states the
  full preference order in one place.
- **Fallbacks are aliases, not dated snapshots.** A dated snapshot retires on a
  published schedule; that is exactly how the current fallback table went stale.
- **The three tiers stay as they are** (see Scoping Findings §3).
- **The cache fix rides along** rather than becoming its own item: same file,
  same call chain, and it is what makes `created_at` one consistent type on both
  the live and cached paths.
- **`SAMPLE_MODELS` is not modified.** Editing the fixture that the existing
  assertions depend on would blur "the fix works" with "the fixture changed."

### Edge cases

- Family with only dated snapshots → newest snapshot by `created_at`. Covered.
- Family absent from the list → `None`, `get_model` falls through to
  `_FALLBACKS`. Unchanged, covered by `test_pick_best_model_no_match`.
- Entry missing `created_at` → sorts as `""` (lowest); the version tuple decides.
- Entry missing `id` → `.get("id", "")`, filtered out by the keyword check.
- `created_at` as a `datetime` vs an ISO string: normalized by `_created_at_key`.
  A list mixing both shapes cannot occur in practice (one shape per source), and
  if one ever did, the version tuple resolves it.
- Substring matching stays as-is: `"opus" in model_id`. Fine for the three
  families in use; a future family whose name contains another's would need a
  stricter match, and that is not today's problem.
- An `LLM_ADMIN_MODEL` / `LLM_HANDLER_MODEL` pin still short-circuits everything
  before any of this runs. Unchanged.

### Testing plan

Summarized in Step 5. The two named regression tests
(`test_pick_best_model_newest_same_length_alias`,
`test_pick_best_model_next_generation_alias`) are the ones that must fail on
current code — the builder should confirm they fail **before** applying the fix,
per `django-mojo-build-conventions`.

### Files touched

- `mojo/helpers/llm.py`
- `tests/test_helpers/llm.py`
- `docs/django_developer/helpers/llm.md`
- `CHANGELOG.md`

No model changes → no `bin/create_testproject` run needed.
